title: Timeline of Foreign IP addresses Bearing Valid US Government TLS Certificates
technique_summary: C2 server uses socat to proxy traffic from listening port to legitimate Treasury.gov IP that does not enforce SNI, reflecting the TLS handshake. Scanner sees valid .gov cert on C2 server without C2 possessing private key. Used for covert exfiltration/C2 under appearance of legitimate Treasury HTTPS.
socat_commands: socat TCP4-LISTEN:51245,fork,reuseaddr TCP4:164.95.89.25:443  # bind C2 port to Treasury IP
socat TCP4-LISTEN:443,fork,reuseaddr TCP4:127.0.0.1:51245     # redirect 443 to C2 daemon
treasury_ip_description: bare Treasury IP serving pki.treasury.gov without SNI enforcement

ip_timeline.notes: Primary confirmed H4 IP. Pre-Jan20 EO appearance.
GSA/FICAM certs on same Aeza IP.
Additional GSA cert on same Aeza IP.
Same GSA cert fingerprint as Aeza, now on Alibaba.
Stark Industries (same entity as Aeza per MINE-003). New IP.
Oracle Cloud South Korea — same ASN as 152.67.204.133. Cert: *.treasury.gov. Confirmed in data/C--russia-aeza-cert-timeline.csv source=russia_aeza_nicetable.
Oracle Cloud SK — linked to www.yszfa.cn via DNS. Korean cloud infrastructure.
Second entry for same Oracle SK IP.
Final entry in table — date only, IP unclear from PDF extraction

bgp_akamai_analysis.summary: Ruled out Akamai auto-peering as explanation. 38 Akamai ASNs checked against target ASNs — no visibility/peering detected on days in question.
bgp_akamai_analysis.methodology: CSV of Akamai-Target ASN pairs, Python script checking RIPEstat Visibility API for unidirectional or mutual visibility.
bgp_akamai_analysis.conclusion: Cert reflection behavior NOT explained by Akamai open peering policy.
bgp_akamai_analysis.source_note: Investigator-conducted analysis using RIPEstat API. Not peer-reviewed.

new_entities.note: 5.180.24.192 (Stark/AS44477) absent from CROSSOVER file — added. 217.142.144.30 corrected to AS31898 Oracle Cloud SK (not Stark). Data source: data/C--russia-aeza-cert-timeline.csv is authoritative.

curator_note: IMPORTANT: This document was authored by the primary investigator as a hypothesis when a Censys researcher suggested CDN noise as explanation for foreign IPs showing .gov certs. The Reflected TLS Attack theory is investigator analysis, NOT a third-party finding. Do NOT cite this document as independent corroboration. T3 tier: contextual/analytical. The underlying raw data (Censys/Shodan observations of foreign IPs with .gov certs) is the T1 evidence — the explanation for the mechanism is still unconfirmed. The IP timeline table within the doc references raw Censys data — those IPs+timestamps should be cross-referenced to the original Censys sources for independent corroboration. New Stark IP 5.180.24.192 not yet in pipeline_ip_crossref — add separately.
reflected_tls_confidence: HYPOTHESIS — not independently confirmed by third party
pre_registration_note: Document written by primary investigator BEFORE statistical analysis was run. Functions as a hypothesis pre-registration: Reflected TLS Attack theory was formulated in response to Censys researcher suggesting CDN noise, before Fisher exact tests confirmed the anomaly statistically. Pre-registration strengthens methodological credibility by showing the mechanism hypothesis preceded the quantitative confirmation. However: the MECHANISM (socat Reflected TLS) remains investigator hypothesis — not yet confirmed by third-party technical analysis or court-grade forensics.
methodological_significance: Pre-registration of mechanism hypothesis (Reflected TLS Attack) before crunching numbers. Subsequent Fisher tests confirm the statistical anomaly (foreign IPs with .gov certs). Pre-reg + stat confirmation = credible H4 mechanism story. Caveat: mechanism itself not independently confirmed — could still be misconfiguration, CDN edge behavior, or other explanation not yet ruled out.
tier_note: T3 contextual — pre-registration document. Underlying Censys data (T1) independently supports the anomaly. Mechanism explanation stays hypothetical until independently confirmed.
